pages.text:
43. MoFT: analysis of volumetric data
Figure 43.1.: (Left) The negative Laplacian (cyan) can be used to map locally concentrated regions of a density
distribution, ρ. (Middle) A bis-crown ether (shown as CPK) binding mode to K+ (violet sphere)
determined using density distributions obtained using RISM and located using Laplacian mapping
in MoFT. (Right) Two level sets of the Laplacian of the K+ distribution, one using a threshold (see
documentation) of 0.1 (solid cyan) and the other using a threshold of 0.01 (semi-transparent cyan).
--convolve Convolution type: (1) Gaussian, (2) box, (3) sinc, (4) Laplacian of Gaussian.
--nlog Take the negative natural logarithm of the input density.
--laplacian Compute Laplacian of the input density using finite difference.
--average Average volumetric data when multiple datasets have been loaded. Otherwise, data will be
accumulated.
43.2. Examples
All files relevant for these examples are available in $AMBERHOME/Ambertools/src/moft/examples/.
Water and ion placement using Laplacian mapping
We will use MoFT to locate and map tightly bound solution particles to a solute molecule of interest using
molecular distribution functions obtained from 3D-RISM. Specifically, we will try to locate K+ binding mode(s)
to a small molecule ionophore - a crown ether.
Generally, molecular density distributions of ions and water have alternating regions where they are highly con-
centrated and others where they are locally depleted. While these complex topologies are a benefit of models that
include particle-particle correlations (such as explicit solvent MD, RISM) they make determination of boundaries
of “binding modes” a complex task. Our solution is to demarcate these binding modes using the Laplacian of the
solvent distributions. When applied to 3D distributions, the Laplacian measures the difference between the local
particle density and the average of the density in a small neighborhood of that point. Hence, where the Laplacian is
positive the local particle density is locally depleted , while for the regions with negative values of the Laplacian the
particle density is locally concentrated . Experience shows that a pre-conditioning using kernels that smooth out
small local variations in the density can help eliminate false positives. Here we will apply the Laplacian mapping
on the density convolution with a 3D Gaussian which can be carried out in a single step by a convolution with a
Laplacian of a Gaussian kernel.
(1) Generate density distributions. Solvent density distributions can be determined by several means, but here
RISM is used (See 7 for how to run RISM). When running RISM, make sure to specify the “-- guv” keyword to
have the solution components density distributions outputted:
950

43.2. Examples
rism3d.snglpnt --prmtop bc5-k.parm7 --xvv KCl-aq-0.2M-pse3.xvv \
--closure pse1,pse2,pse3 --tolerance 1e-03,1e-06 \
--ng 192,192,192 --solvbox 96,96,96 --buffer -1 \
--mdiis_del 0.5 --mdiis_nvec 10 \
--verbose 2 --npropagate 0 --guv g > rism.out
(2) Compute the Laplacian map. First, one has to take the Laplacian of the distribution, using the “convolve”
option:
metatwist --dx g.K+.1.dx.bz2 --odx lp-K+.dx --species K+ K+ --bulkdens 0.2\
--convolve 4 --sigma 1.0
Here, --dx specifies the input density, --odx the root of the output file containing the Laplacian density. Option
“--convolve 4” specifies the type of convolution that leads to the Laplacian; here we have chosen to obtain the
Laplacian using a convolution with the Laplacian of a Gaussian, in this case of width 1.0, specified using “–sigma
1.0”. This step produces a “convolution-lp-K+.dx” file that can be visualized in your molecular graphics application
and will be used in the next step.
(3) Solvent Placement. Second, using the determined Laplacian, we can proceed to the actual analysis:
metatwist --dx g.K+.1.dx.bz2 --ldx convolution-lp-K+.dx --species K+ K+ \
--bulkdens 0.2 --map blobs --thresh 0.1
Here, --ldx specifies the input Laplacian, “--map blobs” asks for solvent placement analysis to be carried out (you
can think about solvent binding modes as blobs) using a Laplacian threshold of 0.1. While all the regions of space
having a negative Laplacian can be considered as “locally concentrated”, often a tighter (more negative) threshold
can simplify the analysis. Lastly, --bulkdens specifies the concentration of the solution particle; in this case K+
has a bulk concentration of 0.2M. With these settings, a pdb file named
“g.K+.1-convolution-lp-K+-blobs-centroid.pdb” is produced that contains the coordinates of the centroid of each
solvation binding mode (in this case only one mode has been found), its occupancy and temperature factor.
ATOM 1 K+ K+ C 1 10.926 12.084 4.026 0.10 92.73 K+
Converting particle density distributions to electron densities
It is often necessary to convert particle density distributions to electron densities to directly compare against
experimentally derived data, such as that obtained from X-ray crystallography. To illustrate this functionality, we
will use the aforementioned RISM calculation on the crown ether immersed in a KCl aqueous solution which
produced density distributions for K+, Cl-, water H and water O. In the first step, each of the particle densities is
converted to their corresponding electron densities using model atomic factors used in crystallography. In a
second stage, all the electron densities are accumulated into a resulting total electron density. Note the use of “
--species” option to guide the choice of model density based on the ionization or oxidation number of each atom
as well as the “ --map rhoel” option to ask for computation of the electron density map. A similar option “--map
rhoelreal” could be used which instead of atomic factors will use reference atomic densities to compute the
overall electron density.
# (1) convert each particle density to electron densities :
metatwist --dx g.K+.1.dx.bz2 --species K+ --odx rho.K+.1.dx --map rhoel --bulkdens 0.2
metatwist --dx g.Cl-.1.dx.bz2 --species Cl- --odx rho.Cl-.1.dx --map rhoel --bulkdens 0.2
metatwist --dx g.O.1.dx.bz2 --species O2- --odx rho.O.1.dx --map rhoel --bulkdens 55.55
# (2) assembly of all densities into rho.dx :
metatwist --dx rho.Cl-.1.dx rho.K+.1.dx rho.O.1.dx --odx rho.dx --species none
951

44. ndfes
44.1. ndfes
The ndfes program [847] reads an input file that describes the biased umbrella sampling, performs the varia-
tional free energy profile (vFEP) [847–849], multistate Bennett acceptance ratio (MBAR) [613], weighted ther-
modynamic perturbation theory (wTP), [850–852], or generalized weighted thermodynamic perturbation theory
(gwTP) [852] methods to obtain an unbiased free energy surface (FES). The FES is output as an XML file (a ndfes
“checkpoint file”). The ndfes program is a part of the FE-ToolKit library. The most recent development version,
extended documentation, and examples can be found online.
It is recommended that one configure Amber with the -DOPENMP cmake option to compile ndfes.OMP, which
parallelizes the calculation with OpenMP.
The ndfes input file, referred to as a “metafile”, lists the “Hamiltonian index” (an integer representing the unbi-
ased potential energy function used to produce the sampling), the temperature, the umbrella potential center(s) and
force constant(s), and a dumpave filename containing the samples to be analyzed. For a N-dimensional surface
generated from M biased simulations, the metafile contains 3+(2*N) columns and M rows.
The first column is an integer (the 0-based Hamiltonian index).
The second column is the temperature, a float, in Kelvin. The temperatures listed in the metafile correspond to
the target temperatures used during the simulation, whereas the --temp command line option to the ndfes is the
temperature of the predicted free energy surface. One must currently run all simulations and calculate the FES at
the same temperature; the mixing of different temperatures is an experimental feature that is not well-tested.
The 3rd column of the metafile is a filename. This is the name of the dumpave file containing the samples.
The 4th column is the position of the umbrella potential center (either in Angstroms or Degrees).
The 5th column is (twice) the harmonic force constant (in kcal/mol/A2or kcal/mol/Deg2), such that the biasing
potential is Ubias(x;x0,k) =k(x−x0)2. This definition of the biasing potential is consistent with the NMR restraints
specified in Amber’s disang files (the value of k=rk2=rk3); however, note that the “disang” files list angular
force constants in kcal/mol/Radian^2. For these restraints, k=�π
1802rk2=�π
1802rk3.
The remaining columns similarly list pairs of umbrella potential centers and (twice the) force constants for the
other dimensions.
The dumpave files read by ndfes are almost the same as the dumpave files produced by Amber. To analyze a N-
dimensional surface with vFEP or MBAR, the dumpave file is expected to contain N+1 columns. The first column
is the time (in ps). The remaining columns are the values of the reaction coordinates. The wTP and gwTP methods
allow one to predict the unbiased FES of a target potential energy function from the biased sampling produced
by one-or-more reference potential energy functions (which may or may not include the target potential energy
function). In this case, the dumpave file contains H extra columns, where H is the number of unique potential
energy functions (either target or reference), and the rows are the unbiased potential energy values of the samples.
The construction of the metafile and the preparation of the corresponding dumpave files is aided by the use of
the ndfes-PrepareAmberData.py script (44.2).
For vFEP:
Usage: ndfes --vfep --order=5 -nquad=5 -c out.chk [options] metafile
For MBAR:
Usage: ndfes --mbar -c out.chk [options] metafile
952

44.1. ndfes
For wTP and gwTP:
Usage: ndfes --mbar --nham=N [--sdos=0.2] -c out.chk [options] metafile
The available options are listed below:
--chkpt,-c This is the output filename. It is an XML file, referred to as a checkpoint file.
--width,-w Bin width size (a float). Use this option multiple times to define the bin width for each dimension.
If only one instance is used, then all dimensions share the same bin width. Recommended values:
0.15 Angstroms and 8.0 degrees. Using small bin widths will result in a large histogram and a
noisy free energy surface. Large bin widths will decrease the resolution of the free energy surface,
and it will underestimate barrier heights.
vFEP-Specific Options:
--nquad,-q Integer number of Gauss-Legendre quadrature points used to integrate the partition function within
each bin. This value is applied to each dimension. [Default: 5]
--order,-o The B-spline order (an integer). A value of 3 is required to yield a smooth FES. Increasing the
order may produce polynomial artifacts from incomplete sampling. [Default: 5]
MBAR-Specific Options:
--nham,-G The integer number of unbiased potential energies to expect in each dumpave file; i.e., this is the
number of extra columns in the dumpave file. This is also the number of free energy surfaces
written to the checkpoint file. [Default: 0, which does not read columns of potential energies, and
it will output a single free energy surface.]
--sdos A float, dU, in reduced energy units (values of kT). If dU > 0, then it will smooth the MBAR
density-of-states using the method described in Hu (2020) [851]. The value used in that paper
was 0.2 kT. [Default: -1. (no smoothing)] One would not normally use this option, but if the free
energy surface contains excessive noise, then one can use this option to see if it makes it more
smooth.
--sepsdos If present, then perform density-of-states smoothing from from the samples of the individual refer-
ence potentials rather than the combined sampling from all potentials. It is arguably more correct
to not use this option, but one may find it yields more stable results in some circumstances.
Options Applicable to both methods:
--temp,-t Temperature. [Default 298.] This must be the same temperature as listed in the metafile. All
simulations must be at the same temperature, and the FES prediction must be at that temperature
as well. The infrastructure for mixing temperatures is in-place, but it is not yet well-tested.
--tol,-T Optimization stopping criteria. [Default: 1.e-9]
--maxit,-n Perform a maximum of maxit iterations. [Default: 99999]
--periodic,-p Integer, which flags a dimension as a periodic coordinate ranging from 0 to 360. The option
can be used multiple times for multiple periodic coordinates. This is a 1-based index, such that
–periodic=1 causes the first reaction coordinate to be treated as periodic. If a periodic value is
outside the range [0,360), it will be wrapped to this range.
--start,-s A float from [0.0,1.0) representing a time as a fraction of the samples. [Default: 0.0] This discards
all samples before “start” as equilibration.
953
pages.section: Angstroms and 8.0 degrees. Using small bin widths will result in a large histogra
pages.section_path: MoFT: analysis of volumetric data > Angstroms and 8.0 degrees. Using small bin widths will result in a large histogra

pages.text:
44. ndfes
--stop,-S A float from (0.0,1.0] representing a time as a fraction of the samples. [Default: 1.0] This discard
the samples after “stop” from the analysis. This is useful when performing convergence tests on
the sampling.
--uniq,-u If present, then prune the inputs to keep only the statistically independent samples. The default
is to keep all samples and estimate errors from block bootstrap analysis, where the block size is
chosen from the statistical inefficiency of the biasing energy.
--maxuniq,-U Integer number of statistically indepedent samples to use from each simulation. To use this
option, you must also use --uniq. [Default: 0, which analyzes all statistically unique samples.]
This option is only useful when trying to determine the smallest subset of data that reproduces the
results from the full set of sampling.
--expert,-X Don’t terminate abnormally if the target and simulation temperatures disagree. It is recom-
mended that all of your simulations are run at the same temperature and you analyze the FES
with that same temperature. The support for mixing different temperatures is still experimental
and likely won’t give you good results. You shouldn’t trust any result you obtain while using this
option.
--nboot,-M Number of bootstrap resamples to estimate errors. [Default: 0] If you want to estimate uncertain-
ties from bootstrap analysis, this should be 20 or larger. Each bootstrap estimate reperforms the
calculation with a subset of the input data using a procedure called “sampling with replacement”.
The error estimate is the standard deviation of the bootstrap calculations. If the biasing potential
time series is correlated, then the error is estimated from a circular block bootstrap procedure,
where the block size is chosen from the biasing potential’s “statistical inefficiency”. In practice,
the bootstrap error estimates are likely an underestimate of the true uncertainty. A better estimate
of the error can be made by performing independent sets (trials) of the simulations with different
thermostat random number seeds and/or different starting coordinates, estimate the free energy
surface from each independent trial, and looking at the variation of the free energy values between
the trials. This can be performed with the ndfes-AvgFESs.py script (44.6).
--btol,-B Optimization tolerance for bootstrap solutions. [Default: 1.e-5] This allows you to reduce the
convergence tolerance when reperforming the calculations during the bootstrap procedure. When
MBAR is used, a value <= 0 will estimate the solution of the MBAR/UWHAM equations by
performing 1 iteration of the MBAR self-consistent equations, rather than a full nonlinear opti-
mization.
44.2. ndfes-PrepareAmberData.py
Prepares the ndfes metafile and dumpave files for several situations, as described below. This script creates a
new dumpave file for a single simulation, and it outputs one line of the metafile to standard output, such that one
would use it in a loop. The primary distinction between ndfes-PrepareAmberData.py and ndfes-CheckEquil.py
is that ndfes-PrepareAmberData.py manipulates the columns of a dumpave file, whereas ndfes-CheckEquil.py
manipulates the rows of a dumpave file. A typical loop structure is shown here.
if [ ! -d analysis/dumpaves ]; then
mkdir -p analysis/dumpaves
fi
for disang in *.disang; do
idump=${disang%.disang}.dumpave
odump=analysis/dumpaves/${idump}
if [ ! -e "${idump}" ]; then
echo "Missing ${idump}" 1>&2
continue;
954
pages.section: Angstroms and 8.0 degrees. Using small bin widths will result in a large histogra
pages.section_path: MoFT: analysis of volumetric data > Angstroms and 8.0 degrees. Using small bin widths will result in a large histogra

pages.text:
44.2. ndfes-PrepareAmberData.py
fi
ndfes-PrepareAmberData.py --prefix=analysis/ --disang=${disang} \
--inp=${idump} --out=${odump} [options]
done > analysis/metafile
cd analysis
[run ndfes using this metafile input]
The exact usage of ndfes-PrepareAmberData.py within the loop depends on what you are trying to accomplish.
The following examples describe several usages.
Example: A 1D FES umbrella window for vFEP or MBAR analysis. There is only 1 collective variable (the
first variable in the disang file). All other restraints are ignored. In other words, in this usage, one
is merely writing a new dumpave that extracts a certain column from the Amber output.
ndfes-PrepareAmberData.py -d rc_0.00.disang -i rc_0.00.dumpave \
-o dumpaves/rc_0.00.dumpave -r 1
The output file will contain 2 columns:
1. The simulation step.
2. The value of the first collective variable.
One would analyze the resulting files with: "ndfes --mbar" or "ndfes --vfep".
Example: A 2D FES umbrella window for vFEP or MBAR analysis. There are 2 collective variable (the
first two variables in the disang file). All other restraints are ignored. In this usage, one is merely
writing a new dumpave that extracts 2 particular columns from the Amber output.
ndfes-PrepareAmberData.py -d rc_0.00_0.00.disang \
-i rc_0.00_0.00.dumpave -o dumpaves/rc_0.00_0.00.dumpave \
-r 1 2
The output file will contain 3 columns:
1. The simulation step .
2. The value of the first collective variable.
3. The value of the second collective variable.
One would analyze the resulting files with: "ndfes --mbar" or "ndfes --vfep".
Example: A 1D FES umbrella window for wTP analysis, in which the effect of a second biasing potential is
removed.
ndfes-PrepareAmberData.py -d rc_0.00.disang -i rc_0.00.dumpave \
-o dumpaves/rc_0.00.dumpave -r 1 -b 2
The output file will contain 4 columns:
1. The simulation step.
2. The value of the first collective variable.
3. The bias energy (kcal/mol) caused by the 2nd collective variable.
4. The potential energy without the bias (which is safely chosen to be 0.0).
One would analyze the resulting files with “ndfes --mbar --nham=2”. The biased FES (the surface
which removes the bias from reaction coordinate 1, but not coordinate 2) would correspond to
"model 0" in the checkpoint file (because it is the first extra column of energies in the dumpave
file), and the unbiased FES would be "model 1" (because it is the second extra column of energies
in the dumpave file). The free energies of “model 1” will only be accurate, in practice, if the
biasing potential introduced by the 2nd reaction coordinate is small (roughly 2 kcal/mol or less).
955
pages.chapter: The potential energy without the bias (which is safely chosen to be 0.0).
pages.section_path: The potential energy without the bias (which is safely chosen to be 0.0).

pages.text:
44. ndfes
Example: A 1D FES umbrella window for wTP analysis, in which a low level Hamiltonian is transformed to
a high level Hamiltonian.
If the simulation was performed with the low level Hamiltonian, then --hamidx is set to 0 to
indicate that the corresponding low level energies are stored in the first instance of --ene.
ndfes-PrepareAmberData.py -d rc_0.00.disang \
--hamidx 0 -i LL_0.00.nc \
-o dumpaves/rc_0.00.dumpave -r 1 \
--ene LL_0.00.mdout --ene HL_0.00.mdout
whereas, if the high-level Hamiltonian produced the trajectory, then --hamidx=1 because the high
level energies are stored in the second instance of --ene.
ndfes-PrepareAmberData.py -d rc_0.00.disang \
--hamidx 1 -i HL_0.00.nc \
-o dumpaves/rc_0.00.dumpave -r 1 \
--ene LL_0.00.mdout --ene HL_0.00.mdout
The output file will contain 4 columns:
1. The simulation step.
2. The value of the first collective variable.
3. The low level potential energy.
4. The high level potential energy.
One would analyze the resulting files with “ndfes --mbar --nham=2”. The low level FES would
correspond to "model 0" and the high level FES would be "model 1".
The mdout files should contain the unbiased potential energies of each sample stored within the
trajectory file. One generates these mdout files by reanalyzing the trajectory with sander using the
-y command line argument and the following options set in the &cntrl section: “irest=0, imin=6,
nstlim=0, nmropt=0, ntpr=1, ntwx=0, ntwf=0”.
Example: This the same as the previous example; however, the low-level simulations were restart several
times to yield several trajectory and reanalysis files. The individual restarts are combined by
including multiple filenames within a single instance of an option.
ndfes-PrepareAmberData.py -d rc_0.00.disang \
--hamidx 0 -i LL_0.00.nc LL_0.00_rst1.nc LL_0.00_rst2.nc \
-o dumpaves/rc_0.00.dumpave -r 1 \
--ene LL_0.00.mdout LL_0.00_rst1.mdout LL_0.00_rst2.mdout \
--ene HL_0.00.mdout HL_0.00_rst1.mdout HL_0.00_rst2.mdout
The output file will contain 4 columns:
1. The simulation step.
2. The value of the first collective variable.
3. The low level potential energy.
4. The high level potential energy.
One would analyze the resulting files with “ndfes --mbar --nham=2”. The low level FES would
correspond to "model 0" and the high level FES would be "model 1".
Example: This is similar to the previous wTP examples; however, an additional biasing potential is removed
from the 1D FES.
956

44.2. ndfes-PrepareAmberData.py
ndfes-PrepareAmberData.py -d rc_0.00.disang \
--hamidx 1 -i HL_0.00.nc \
-o dumpaves/rc_0.00.dumpave -r 1 -b 2 \
--ene LL_0.00.mdout --ene HL_0.00.mdout
The output file will contain 6 columns:
1. The simulation step.
2. The value of the first collective variable.
3. The biased low level potential energy.
4. The biased high level potential energy.
5. The unbiased low level potential energy.
6. The unbiased high level potential energy.
One would analyze the resulting files with “ndfes --mbar --nham=4”. The biased low level FES
would correspond to "model 0". The biased high level FES would be "model 1". The unbiased
low level FES would be "model 2". The unbiased high level FES would be "model 3".
The available optional arguments are provided below.
-d,--disang The disang file defining the restraint energies.
-T,--temp Temperature [Default: 298.]
-i,--inp The input dumpave file(s) or netcdf trajectory file(s). Multiple files can be listed for each instance
of --inp, which are interpretted as a series of restarts. The output will aggregate the samples from
each restart.
-o,--out The output dumpave file to write.
-r,--rxn The column indexes of the desired reaction coordinates. The number of columns defines the
dimensionality of the free energy surface. This is a 1-based integer, such that -r 1 corresponds to
the first reaction coordinate. The output dumpave file will only contain the reaction coordinates
specified by this option.
-b,--bias The column indexes of the biased coordinates. This is a 1-based integer such that -b 1 corresponds
to the first reaction coordinate. The reaction coordinates specified by this option will not appear in
the output dumpave file. Instead, extra columns of energies will be written so that the biased and
unbiased free energies will be computed in the reduced dimensional space of coordinates specified
by the -r option.
-e,--ene The mdout file(s) containing the unbiased potential energy of each frame. Multiple files can be
listed for each instance of --ene, which are interpretted as a series of restarts. Each instance of
--ene begins a list of mdout files for a new Hamiltonian. The ordering of the mdouts must be
consistent with the ordering of the --inp files. This option is only used when performing wTP or
gwTP analysis.
-H,--hamidx The index of the Hamiltonian that produced the trajectory. [Default: 0] The value listed here will
be written as the first column in the metafile.
-s,--sym If present, negate the observed samples of the specified reaction coordinate. This is only useful for
1D free energy surfaces of symmetric systems, and it should not be used in any other circumstance.
It can be used to symmetrize the sampling to ensure that the free energy surface is symmetric about
the origin.
957
pages.chapter: The unbiased high level potential energy.
pages.section_path: The unbiased high level potential energy.

pages.text:
44. ndfes
-w,--width The histogram bin width. Use this option multiple times for multiple dimensions. If used only
used once, then it assumes each dimension uses the same width. If used multiple times, it must
be used the same number of times as the --rxn option. If this option is used, then the output
samples will be shifted by +1.e-6 if it would lie exactly on the border of two bins. This is only
useful when creating dumpave files for systems that will use the --sym option, because negating a
sample precisely on the border between two bins does not round the bin-index ’away from zero’
in a manner required to perfectly enforce the desired symmetry.
-P,--prefix Remove the leading string from the output dumpave filename when writing the metafile. If
one is trying to store the dumpaves in analysis/dumpaves/*.dumpave and write a metafile called
analysis/metafile, such that the contents of the metafile refer to the dumpaves with the prefix
dumpaves/*.dumpave, then set --prefix=”analysis/” to strip the leading directory when writing
the dumpave filesnames to the metafile.
44.3. ndfes-CheckEquil.py
This script performs forward and reverse analysis of the biasing potential time series to detect the equilibrated
region of a trajectory. It optionally uses cpptraj to write a new trajectory containing the statistically independent
samples from the equilibrated region. One would normally either read an input NetCDF trajectory file and output
a new trajectory file with fewer frames, or read an input dumpave file and output a new dumpave file with fewer
rows. One would typically use this script before using ndfes-PrepareAmberData.py (44.2).
Examples that analyze NetCDF trajectory files The biasing potential is evaluated using the coordinates read
from the trajectory file and the restraints defined in the disang file, but only for those restraints listed with the -r
option.
Example: Exclude the first 25% of a simulation and keep all remaining samples.
ndfes-CheckEquil.py -p sys.parm7 -i inp.nc -o out.nc \
--start=0.25 --skipg --skipsearch -d inp.disang -r 1
Example: Automatically detect equilibration from forward and reverse analysis, keeping all samples in the
production region.
ndfes-CheckEquil.py -p sys.parm7 -i inp.nc -o out.nc \
--skipg -d inp.disang -r 1
Example: Automatically detect equilibration from forward and reverse analysis, keeping only the statistically
independent samples within the production region.
ndfes-CheckEquil.py -p sys.parm7 -i inp.nc -o out.nc \
-d inp.disang -r 1
Example: Automatically detect equilibration but skip the writing of a new trajectory.
ndfes-CheckEquil.py -i inp.nc -d inp.disang -r 1
Note: If the simulations were restart several times, then one can specify multiple input trajectories to reconstruct
the time series; e.g., -i init.nc rst1.nc rst2.nc [...]
958
pages.chapter: The unbiased high level potential energy.
pages.section_path: The unbiased high level potential energy.

pages.text:
44.3. ndfes-CheckEquil.py
Examples that analyze dumpave files The biasing potential is evaluated from the disang file and the samples
listed in the input dumpave file for those reaction coordinates listed with the -r option.
Example: Exclude the first 25% of a simulation and keep all remaining samples.
ndfes-CheckEquil.py -i inp.dumpave -o out.dumpave \
--start=0.25 --skipg --skipsearch \
-d inp.disang -r 1
Example: Automatically detect the production region from from forward and reverse analysis, but keep all
remaining samples.
ndfes-CheckEquil.py -i inp.dumpave -o out.dumpave \
--skipg -d inp.disang -r 1
Example: Automatically detect equilibration from forward and reverse analysis, and keep only the statisti-
cally independent samples.
ndfes-CheckEquil.py -i inp.dumpave -o out.dumpave \
-d inp.disang -r 1
Example: Automatically detect equilibration but skip the writing of a new dumpave.
ndfes-CheckEquil.py -i inp.dumpave -d inp.disang -r 1
Note: If the simulations were restart several times, then one can specify multiple input dumpaves to reconstruct
the timeseries; e.g., -i init.dumpave rst1.dumpave [...]
The standard output will show several columns of quantities with the following meanings.
1. The first column is the name of the input dumpave (or trajectory) file.
2. The second column is the number of input samples.
3. The number of output samples.
4. The percentage of samples to be excluded as the equilibration region.
5. The index of the first sample to be included in the production region.
6. The stride used to extract the statistically independent samples from the production region. (This will be 1
if --skipg is specified.) The values from columns 5 and 6 correspond to the “start” and “offset” arguments of
the “trajout” command in cpptraj to extract a new, petite trajectory file.
7. The average value of the biasing potential in the first half of the production region.
8. The standard error of the biasing potential values in the first half of the production region.
9. The average value of the biasing potential in the second half othe production region.
10. The standard error of the biasing potential values in the second half of the production region.
11. The last, optional, column displays warnings that may require your attention. “UNCONVERGED” means
that a production region could not be identified. “Teq > 70” means that more than 70% of the simulation
should be thrown away as equilibration. “N<50” means that there are fewer than 50 samples being output.
959
pages.chapter: The last, optional, column displays warnings that may require your attention. “UN
pages.section_path: The last, optional, column displays warnings that may require your attention. “UN

pages.text:
44. ndfes
The available optional arguments are provided below.
-d,--disang The disang file defining the restraint energies.
-i,--inp INP The input netcdf trajectory (or disang) file(s). Multiple files can be listed for each instance of
--inp, which are interpretted as a series of restarts.
-p,--parm Amber parm7 (only needed if --out is a netcdf file).
-o,--out The output trajectory (or disang) file.
-r,--rxn The indexes of the desired reaction coordinates. This is a 1-based index, such the -r 1 refers to the
first restraint defined in the disang file.
--start Exclude the first fraction of samples. This is a float from 0. to 1. [Default: 0.0]
--stop Ignore the last fraction of samples. This is a float from 0. to 1. [Default: 1.0]
-m,--maxeq The maximum fraction of samples to exclude as equilibration [Default: 0.75]
-g,--skipg If present, keep all samples after identifying the production region (it assumes g=1, where g is the
statistical inefficiency.)
-S,--skipsearch Skip the auto-detection of the production region. Instead, extract the samples from –start
to –stop.
-C,--chunks The integer number of chunks to split the data into to perform time series analysis [Default: 20].
In other words, the timeseries is split into N chunks, and the detection algorithm tries to figure out
how many of the first chunks should be discarded as equilibration.
--ptol The p-value threshold when performing statistical tests. Larger values exclude more samples.
Some useful values to note: 0.21135667595 (79% confidence, 1.25 sigma) 0.31729526862 (68%
confidence, 1.00 sigma, default) 0.45310971043 (55% confidence, 0.75 sigma) 0.61678615958
(38% confidence, 0.50 sigma). [Default: 0.31729526862]
--prune Try to find a minimal number of samples to represent the production region that reproduces the
average value of the production region by adjusting the stride. Only available if -S is not used.
--minsize The minimum allowable number of samples after pruning. [Default: 75]
--maxsize The maximum allowable number of samples after pruning. [Default: 125]
44.4. ndfes-CombineMetafiles.py
Creates a new metafile from one-or-more metafiles. This is primarily used to analyze the aggregate sampling
from multiple, independent trials; or multiple iterations of the finite temperature string method. It can also be used
to move a metafile to another directory without needing to move the dumpave files that it refers to (the script will
adjust the relative path of the filenames in the output metafile).
Usage: ndfes-CombineMetafiles.py -o output input [inputs ...]
960
pages.chapter: The last, optional, column displays warnings that may require your attention. “UN
pages.section_path: The last, optional, column displays warnings that may require your attention. “UN

pages.text:
44.5. ndfes-PrintFES.py
44.5. ndfes-PrintFES.py
Prints the free energies defined in a ndfes checkpoint at the histogram bin centers, for those bins occupied by at
least 1 sample.
Usage: ndfes-PrintFES.py [-m MODEL] [-c] chkpt
The output consists of N+5 columns:
1. Columns 1-to-N are the reaction coordinate values of the bin center.
2. Column N+1 is the free energy value (kcal/mol).
3. Column N+2 is the standard error of the free energy value (kcal/mol). If -c is used as an option, then this
value is a 95% confidence interval (the standard error scaled by 1.96).
4. Column N+3 is the reweighting entropy. The entropy is a measure of the uniformity of MBAR weights
within the bin, such that it is 1.0 if the weights are uniform and 0.0 if the weights are dominated by only a
few samples. Entropies below 0.6 likely mean that the free energy estimate for this bin is untrustworthy. The
entropy values are not computed when performing vFEP.
5. Column N+4 is the integer number of samples within the bin.
The available optional arguments are provided below.
-m,--model The zero-based index of the model to print. [Default: -1, which causes all models within the
checkpoint file to be printed.]
-c,--ci If present, write the 95% confidence intervals rather than standard errors (the 95% confidence
interval is 1.96 * standard error).
44.6. ndfes-AvgFESs.py
Averages 1-or-more models from the supplied checkpoint file(s) and writes a new checkpoint file. If N input
surfaces are supplied, the output checkpoint file will contain N+1 surfaces. The first surface will be the averaged
free energy, and the remaining N surfaces will be the same as the input surfaces, but uniformly shifted to minimize
the weighted least square fit to the average.
Example: To estimate the uncertainty in the free energy values, one often performs multiple sets of inde-
pendent simulations (trials), and one can use ndfes to analyze each trial. Furthermore, one can
use ndfes-CombineMetafiles.py to aggregate the sampling and make a best estimate of the free
energies from all available sampling. One then uses ndfes-AvgFESs.py to update the uncertainty
of the best estimate by considering the variations between the independent trials. This example is
the recommended usage.
cd Trial1 && ndfes.OMP --mbar -w 0.15 --nboot=20 \
-c chkpt metafile && cd ..
cd Trial2 && ndfes.OMP --mbar -w 0.15 --nboot=20 \
-c chkpt metafile && cd ..
cd Trial3 && ndfes.OMP --mbar -w 0.15 --nboot=20 \
-c chkpt metafile && cd ..
ndfes-CombineMetafiles.py -o Aggregate/metafile Trial1/metafile \
Trial2/metafile Trial3/metafile
cd Aggregate && ndfes.OMP --mbar -w 0.15 --nboot=20 \
-c chkpt metafile && cd ..
ndfes-AvgFESs.py --fes Trial1/chkpt --fes Trial2/chkpt \
--fes Trial3/chkpt --ene Aggregate/chkpt \
-o Aggregate/chkpt.BetterErrorEstimates
961
pages.chapter: Column N+4 is the integer number of samples within the bin.
pages.section_path: Column N+4 is the integer number of samples within the bin.

pages.text:
44. ndfes
The available optional arguments are provided below.
--fes One or more models to average; e.g., chkptfile=0 will read model 0 from the checkpoint file names
’chkptfile’. If the trailing ’=idx’ is missing, then ’=0’ is assumed.
--ene If present, then read the energies from this checkpoint file and use the --fes surfaces only to update
the error estimate. The only difference between this option and --ref is: this option reports the
reference energy rather than the average energy; i.e., the output model with --ene only modifies
the standard errors. This option also uses the chkptfile=i syntax. You should always be using
this option. If you have multiple free energy surfaces, you will make a better estimate of the free
energy values by analyzing the aggregate sampling.
--ref If present, then shift the --fes surfaces to minimize the error with respect to this reference surface,
but report the average energy instead of the reference energy. This option also uses the chkptfile=i
syntax. The --ene option should be preferred over the use of --ref.
-o,--out The output checkpoint file name.
44.7. ndfes-path
This script has 3 use cases:
1. Evaluate the free energy of a multidimensional surface along a parametric curve.
2. Optimize a minimum free energy pathway through a fixed, multidimensional surface.
3. Prepare a new set of simulation inputs for the finite temperature string method. To better understand how to
use ndfes-path to perform the finite temperature string method, you should refer to the FE-ToolKit tutorial
available online.
It is recommended that one configure Amber with the -DOPENMP cmake option to compile ndfes-path.OMP,
which parallelizes the calculations with OpenMP.
Example: Generate the next directory of simulations using modified string method described in Rosta (2011)
[853]. This method does not read a free energy surface from a ndfes checkpoint file. This is the
recommended string method when you have more than 5 reaction coordinates.
ndfes-path -d DISANG -i MDIN --curit CURIT --maxit=0 --msm --no-smooth --linear
You can also choose a spline representation, either: --akima or --linear By setting --maxit=0, the
new spline will be constructed from the observed reaction coordinate averages obtained from simu-
lation (by averaging the appropriate columns in the amber dumpave output file). The discretization
of the path must be the same as the number of simulations. It is recommended to use the --linear
spline representation of the path when performing the modified string method.
Example: Generating the next directory of simulations using the modified string method accelerated with
“synthetic simulations”. This is similar to the modified string method, but the minimum free
energy path is optimized on the free energy surface. This version of the modified string method
reads a free energy surface from a ndfes checkpoint file to perform the optimization; therefore, it
is only appropriate when you have a few reaction coordinates.
ndfes-path -d DISANG -i MDIN --curit CURIT --maxit=300 --msm
By settings --maxit>0, the new spline will be constructed by performing minimizations on the
free energy surface in the reduced space of reaction coordinates. In this manner, the output path
will be the minimum free energy pathway along the current estimate of the unbiased free energy
962
pages.chapter: Prepare a new set of simulation inputs for the finite temperature string method.
pages.section_path: Prepare a new set of simulation inputs for the finite temperature string method.

pages.text:
44.7. ndfes-path
surface. The ’synthetic modified string method’ described here has not been previously published;
in part because our experience has been that ’grid based string method’ is a superior approach.
The discretization of the path does not have to be the same as the number of simulations. You can
set this using, for example, --npathpts=100. For this reason, one may prefer to use --akima rather
than --linear to represent the path.
Example: Generate the next directory of simulations using the “surface accelerated string method” (unpub-
lished, in prep.). This is the recommended string method when you have 5 or fewer reaction
coordinates.
ndfes-path -d DISANG -i MDIN --curit CURIT --sasm
The discretization of the path does not have to be the same as the number of simulations. You can
set this using, for example, --npathpts=100. For this reason, one may prefer to use --akima rather
than --linear to represent the path.
Example: Print a 1D projection through a multidimensional FES.
ndfes-path --ipath IPATH --chk CHK --opath OPATH --maxit=0 \
[--nsplpts NSP]
Example: Optimize a path on a fixed FES without generating a new directory of simulations.
ndfes-path --ipath IPATH --chk CHK --opath OPATH --maxit MAXIT \
[--npathpts NPTS] [--nsplpts NSP]
Options controlling the creation of new simulation inputs
-d,--disang Template disang file. A disang file is considered a template when some of the r2 and r3 values
are replaced with a placeholder string; e.g., r2=RC1, r3=RC1, which denote the first, second, etc.
reaction coordinates.
-i,--mdin Template mdin file. It is expected that the mdin file sets nmropt=1. When writing mdin files from
this template, it replaces the DUMPA VE= and DISANG= lines to refer to the appropriate restraint
file.
--curit Index of the current iteration. The current directory is assumed to be itXXX, where XXX is a
zero-padded integer, specified by --curit. If --curit=0, then the directory name is assumed to be
“init”.
--odir Output directory. If unspecified, it is itXXX, where XXX is 0-padded integer corresponding to
--curit + 1. The program will not create the directory; the directory must exist.
--pad The width of the file and directory name 0-padding. [Default: 3]
--dry-run Do not write any output files to disk
--neqit Remove the first N string iterations as equilibration in later iterations. [Default: -1]. The value of
N is the last iteration to be excluded, such that N=0 would only exclude the simulation data in the
init directory. This option only effects the simulation restart files that can be copied to the next
directory. You will need to set the --neqit option in ndfes-FTSM-PrepareAnalysis.py to effect the
FES.
--cp-nearest If present, then the restart of the next iteration image is copied from the image whose centroid
was closest to the new window center. If the grid-based string method is not being used, then the
default behavior is to copy the restarts from the corresponding image.
963
pages.chapter: Prepare a new set of simulation inputs for the finite temperature string method.
pages.section_path: Prepare a new set of simulation inputs for the finite temperature string method.

pages.text:
44. ndfes
--prefix The subdirectory to read and write mdin and rst7 files. By default, these files are written to itXXX/,
but setting a --prefix=SUBDIR will write them to itXXX/SUBDIR/. This is useful when optimiz-
ing several paths on a common FES.
--extra-prefix Extra subdirectories to search for rst7 files. This is only useful when trying to optimize
several paths at the same time. By using this option, one can copy the restart file from any of the
paths. This option can be used more than once.
Options for path analysis and optimization without creating new simulation inputs
--chk Read the free energy surface from the specified ndfes checkpoint file. This option must be used
with --ipath and --opath, and it cannot be used with --curit --mdin and --disang.
--ipath Read the umbrella centers and force constants from the specified metafile. IPATH can also be
a table whose first 2 columns are ignored. Columns 3-to-3+Ndim are assumed to be umbrella
centers. All remaining columns are ignored. In this case, all force constants are assumed to be
200 kcal/mol/Ang**2. IPATH can also be a ndfes path XML file, from which it will read the last
path. This option must be used with --chk and --opath, and it cannot be used with --curit --mdin
and --disang.
--opath Write the output path to OPATH. This file will contain 2+2*Ndim columns: i, t[i],
rc[i][dim], and fc[i][dim]. Two additional files will be written: OPATH.xml (the path iter-
ations) and OPATH.interp.model.dat (A file containing the path properties). The columns of
OPATH.interp.model.dat correspond to: i t[i] rc[i][dim] F[i] dF[i] RE[i] N[i], where t is the
progress variable, rc is the reaction coordinate, F is the free energy, Err is the standard error in
F, RE is the reweighting entropy, and N is the number of samples in the bin that this point lies
within. This option must be used with --ipath and --opath, and it cannot be used with --curit
--mdin and --disang.
Options for reading a free energy surface
-m,--model The model index within the checkpoint to read. [Default: 0]
--minsize If a FES bin has fewer than MINSIZE samples, then discard it. [Default: 0, Recommend: 10]
Options for representing the free energy surface
--wavg Represent the FES by a weighted average of nearby bin values using B-splines. This argument
requires an integer, which is the B-spline order used to perform the averaging. [Default: 0]
--wavg-niter Number of iterative corrections to the wavg histogram values such that the interpolated values
match the values at the bin centers. This will correct artifacts introduced from the averaging
procedure, but it will also reproduce noise in the data. [Default: 0].
--rbf Represent the FES with Radial Basis Function interpolation. Valid for both vFEP and MBAR
--shape The RBF shape parameter used when --rbf is specified. Small values tend to yield larger oscilla-
tions. [Default: 100.0]
--arbf Represent the FES with an approximate radial basis function parametrized from the central bin
and its nearest neighbors. This option requires an integer, which is the number of nearby layers
to include in the RBF solutions. [Default: -1, which disables the arbf]. A value of 1 constructs
a RBF from the central bin and first layer of nearby neighbors. This would be fast, but noticable
artifacts would be present in the FES. A value of 2 reduces the artifacts. Setting ARBF >= 3 is not
reccommended because it would likely be more expensive than simply using --rbf.
964
pages.chapter: Prepare a new set of simulation inputs for the finite temperature string method.
pages.section_path: Prepare a new set of simulation inputs for the finite temperature string method.

pages.text:
44.7. ndfes-path
--bspl Cardinal B-spline interpolation. Valid only for vFEP, which is the default when the surface was
obtained with the vFEP method.
--hist No interpolation; only use the histogram values. Path optimizations will not be possible with this
option.
Recommended options for representing the free energy surface
• To smooth out a noisey FES (this is the recommended set of options for representing a FES): --wavg=4
--wavg-niter=1 --minsize=10
• To better reproduce the noise: --wavg=4 --wavg-niter=5 --minsize=10
• To exactly reproduce the noise: --rbf --minsize=10
Options for representing the parametric curve
--no-smooth Do not smooth the spline control points when generating a new path. The default is to apply
smoothing procedure.
--linear Use piecewise linear splines. This is the default when using --msm --maxit=0.
--akima Use Akima splines. This is usually the default, except when --msm --maxit=0.
--fix0 If present, then don’t update the position of t=0 (the beginning of the path).
--fix1 If present, then don’t update the position of t=1 (the end of the path).
--npathpts Reconstruct a new initial guess from IPATH using N points. This is useful when making a simple
guess but you want to optimize a path with more points. The default is to optimize with the same
number of points as found in the IPATH file. If this option is unused and one is creating a new
directory of simulations with --gbsm, then it will default to 100.
--nsplpts Given a spline of the final path, interpolate the spline with N points to write
OPATH.interp.model.dat. To print the free energy values at the input points, set: --maxit=0 and do
not use --nsplpts nor --npathpts.
Options for optimizing the path
--maxit The maximum number of synthetic iterations used to find the minimum free energy path on the
fixed surface. If one is generating a new directory of simulations, then the default is 300. If one is
analyzing a 1D projection, then the default is 0.
--ptol T-test p-value tolerance used to terminate the optimization of the synthetic path optimization on
the free energy surface. Values close to (but less than) 1.0 represent a strict tolerance requiring
many iterations to achieve converence. A value >= 1 will never converge. A value <= 0 will
prematurely terminate the optimization. [Default: 0.95]
--acc-oobk The force constant used to push the path toward the nearest occupied bin, if it is out-of-bounds.
[Default: 300]
--acc-maxit The number of nonlinear sythetic optimizations steps. [Default: 100]
--distol FES-FTSM slope termination tolerance for distance coordinates [Default: 0.05 Ang].
--angtol ANGTOL FES-FTSM slope termination tolerance for angle coordinates [Default: 0.5 Deg].
--mlen The number of iterations to consider when calculating the slope of the path with respect to iteration
[Default: 5].
965
pages.chapter: Prepare a new set of simulation inputs for the finite temperature string method.
pages.section_path: Prepare a new set of simulation inputs for the finite temperature string method.

pages.text:
44. ndfes
Options for altering the predicted force constants
--maxfc Maximum allowable force constants for distance restraints. This value is the largest force constant
that you feel comfortable simulating. [Default: 300 kcal/mol/A^2]
--minfc Minimum allowable force constants for distance restraints. This value is the smallest force con-
stant that you feel comfortable simulating. [Default: 50 kcal/mol/A^2]
--maxfc-ang Maximum allowable force constants for angle restraints. This value is the largest force constant
that you feel comfortable simulating. [Default: 0.5 kcal/mol/deg^2]
--minfc-ang Minimum allowable force constants for angle restraints. This value is the smallest force constant
that you feel comfortable simulating. [Default: 0.1 kcal/mol/deg^2]
--predfc Adjust the simulation force constants and progress variable values to place simulations along the
path in a manner such that the expected centroid means are evenly spaced (rather than spacing the
umbrella window centers). By default, the force constants are not adjusted and the simulations
are chosen to uniformly space the biasing potential centers. This option should not be used when
performing the grid-based string method.
--smoothfc If present, apply smoothing to the predicted force constants. This option should not be used when
performing the grid-based string method.
--tdispfc Adjust the force constants to achieve the target displacement. Only used if --pred-fc is present.
A value of 1 will scale the force constants such that the predicted centroids will displace along
the path by the same amount as the nearest neighbors of a uniformly discretized set of windows.
A value of 0.5 will adjust the force constants so that the centroid does not displace by more than
1/2 the distance between nearest neighbors. Setting tdisp to small values will generally cause the
force constants to increase. [Default: 0.75] This option should not be used when performing the
grid-based string method.
Misc options
-T,--temp Temperature, K [Default: 298.]
--print-freq Output frequency during optimization [Default: 50]
44.8. ndfes-path-prepguess.py
This script helps to create an initial guess from which one can start the finite temperature string method. It is
presumed that the user has prepared and equilibrated one-or-more structures that approximate stationary point(s)
on the reduced dimensional free energy surface. One needs to have prepared at least one structure (typically either
the reactant or product structure). By “reactant” and “product” we mean the two ends of a minimum free energy
pathway. Furthermore, it is presumed that the user has prepared two-or-more disang files that the initial path should
pass through. One needs at least 2 disang files to represent the two ends of the path. Given these disang and restart
files, the script writes a series of NSIM output mdin and output disang files that connect the input disang files
via linear interpolation. Furthermore, one-or-more shell scripts will be created that can be used to generate initial
starting configurations for each of the NSIM states via a sequential series of short simulations from the nearest
input restart file.
The available options are listed below.
-d,--disang A template disang file.
-i,--mdin A template mdin file.
966
pages.chapter: Prepare a new set of simulation inputs for the finite temperature string method.
pages.section_path: Prepare a new set of simulation inputs for the finite temperature string method.

pages.text:
44.9. ndfes-path-analyzesims.py
--min Either a disang defining a proposed stationary point on the free energy surface. The script will
check to see if there is a corresponding restart file by replacing the ’.disang’ suffix with a ’.rst7’
suffix. This option must be used at least twice (once for the reactant and product, repsectively),
and a corresponding restart file (with velocities) must exist for at least one state.
-n,--nsim Number of windows in the string.
--pad Length of the filename padding. [Default: 3] The files will be named imgXXX.mdin and
imgXXX.disang, where XXX is a zero-padded integer.
-q,--dry-run Don’t prepare the outputs; only show what would be done.
-o,--odir Output directory to write the files.
44.9. ndfes-path-analyzesims.py
This script is used during the finite temperature string method to prepare the lastest set of simulations for analy-
sis. A typical string iteration involves the following steps:
1. Perform the simulations.
2. Prepare the sampling for analysis using ndfes-path-analyzesims.py. This effectively acts as a convenience
wrapper to ndfes-CheckEquil.py, ndfes-PrepareAmberData.py, and ndfes-CombineMetafiles.py.
3. Run ndfes to obtain the current estimate of the free energy surface from the aggregate sampling obtained
from the current and previous string iterations.
4. Run ndfes-path to optimize a minimum free energy path on the current estimate of the free energy surface
and write a new directory of simulation inputs.
The available options are listed below.
-d,--disang Template disang file
--prefix The subdirectory within the iteration directory containing the simulation data. The default prefix
is ”, which will search for itXXX/img*.dumpave. If --prefix=SUBDIR is set, then it will search
for itXXX/SUBDIR/img*.dumpave.
--curit Index of the current iteration. The current directory is assumed to be itXXX, where XXX is a
zero-padded integer, specified by --curit. If --curit=0, then the directory name is assumed to be
“init”.
--pad Length of the directory name zero-padding. [Default: 3]
--nprev The number of previous iterations to include in the estimation of the free energy surface. A value
of 0 means only the sampling from the current iteration contributes the calculation of the free
energy surface. A value of 1000 will include all previous iterations. [Default: 1000]
-T,--temp Temperature, K [Default: 298.] This is the temperature value written to the metafile. It should be
the same temperaure used when running ndfes.
--start Exclude the first percentage of samples [Default: 0.0]
--stop Ignore the samples after this percentage [Default: 1.0]
--maxeq Maximum fraction of samples to exclude as equilibration [Default: 0.7]
-g,--skipg If present, keep all samples after identifying the production region (assume g=1, where g is the
statistical inefficiency).
967
pages.chapter: Run ndfes-path to optimize a minimum free energy path on the current estimate of
pages.section_path: Run ndfes-path to optimize a minimum free energy path on the current estimate of

pages.text:
44. ndfes
-S,--skipsearch If present, skip the forward/reverse analysis search for a production region and instead
extract the region specified by -start and -stop.
--neqit Phase-out the first neqit iterations from the sampling when generating metafile.all as curit in-
creases. [Default: -1, which does not phase-out any early iterations.]
968
